summary: Doxycycline can cause a decrease in the absorption of Ferric sulfate resulting in a reduced serum concentration and potentially a decrease in efficacy.
extended_description: A number of studies have demonstrated that the absorption of tetracycline could be decreased by 40 to 70% with the concurrent use of ferrous sulfate 100 to 300 mg [A35571, A35572, A35573, A35574]. The mechanism behind this kind of interaction is most likely linked to the formation of tetracycline-iron chelates in the gastrointestinal tract. These chelates are in turn, poorly absorbed, which results in impaired absorption and decreased concentrations of both the tetracycline and iron agents. Conversely, investigations with doxycycline have shown that oral iron agents may only decrease doxycycline concentrations by about 17 to 45%, regardless if the iron is administered a number of hours after doxycycline, and the half-life of intravenously administered doxycycline was decreased by an average of 34% when given concurrently with oral iron [A35578, A35580]. The fact that this occurs even after a temporal separation of the administration of oral iron and/or the use of intravenous doxycycline is that the administered doxycycline could undergo intestinal secretion and reabsorption, both of which are activities that can be modified by chelation of the doxycycline with iron agents in the gastrointestinal tract. Otherwise, no significant interaction occurs when iron is given at least 3 hours prior to or 2 hours after tetracycline administration [A35577]. Finally, other studies still note that the combined administration of tetracyclines and iron agents could reduce iron absorption by an average of 49 to 78% in humans [A35574, A35585].
management: No significant interaction occurs when iron is given at least 3 hours prior to or 2 hours after tetracycline administration [A35577].

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Sitagliptin can be decreased when used in combination with Olanzapine.
extended_description: Agents that directly or indirectly cause hyperglycaemia as an adverse event may alter the pharmacological response and the therapeutic actions of blood glucose lowering agents when co-administered. Mechanism of the interaction may vary, including decreased insulin secretion, increased adrenaline release, reduced total body potassium, negative effect on glucose metabolism, and drug-induced weight gain leading to increased tissue resistance.[A37331, A36662] Decreased hypoglycaemic effects of antidiabetic therapy may require increased dosage.
management: Monitor blood glucose levels of patients receiving medications to lower blood glucose levels during concomitant use of other drugs that may potentially cause hyperglycaemia. Dosage adjustments may be needed as necessary, and if this is the case, refer to the instructions on the individual drug label for the blood glucose lowering agent.

summary: The risk or severity of hypoglycemia can be increased when Pregabalin is combined with Sitagliptin.
extended_description: Both the subject and the affected drug are associated with hypoglycemia, either by design (i.e. antihyperglycemics in diabetes) or as part of an adverse effect profile. The use of multiple medications that can lower blood glucose may lead to an additive blood glucose-lowering effect and subsequent symptomatic hypoglycemia.[A37413,A37414]
management: Despite the fact that the routine use of multiple hypoglycemia-associated agents is common in the setting of diabetes, caution should still be exercised when initiating multiple medications with this ability. Diabetic patients who test their blood glucose levels at home should be advised to test more frequently when initiating new hypoglycemic medications and to initiate appropriate measures should hypoglycemia manifest. Patients without the ability to monitor blood glucose directly can still self-monitor for hypoglycemia by watching carefully for outward signs or symptoms (e.g. sweating, shaking, weakness/fatigue). Frequent symptomatic hypoglycemia may warrant dose decreases and/or discontinuation of the culprit medication in both diabetic and non-diabetic patients.

Frequent symptomatic hypoglycemia may warrant dose decreases and/or discontinuation of the culprit medication in both diabetic and non-diabetic patients.

summary: Calcium carbonate can cause a decrease in the absorption of Rosuvastatin resulting in a reduced serum concentration and potentially a decrease in efficacy.
extended_description: The co-administration of rosuvastatin with antacids containing aluminum and/or magnesium hydroxides has been shown to decrease exposure to rosuvastatin by approximately 50%.[A187514,L6967] This pharmacokinetic interaction may be the result of impaired rosuvastatin absorption due to increases in gastric pH or, alternatively, due to direct binding of rosuvastatin by aluminum and/or magnesium hydroxide.[A187514] It is currently unclear whether this interaction applies to other statins or antacid preparations.[A187514]
management: As antacids are relatively short-acting, this interaction can be largely mitigated by ensuring antacids are taken at least 2 hours _after_ administration of rosuvastatin.

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Dextromethorphan is combined with Olanzapine.
extended_description: Serotonin modulators often inhibit cytochrome CYP2D6 in the liver. This enzyme metabolizes several antipsychotics. This may lead to increased adverse effects of the antipsychotics metabolized by CYP2D6.
management: Avoid concomitant use of serotonin modulators an antipsychotic medications. Adjust antipsychotic medication dosages as needed.

summary: The bioavailability of Budesonide can be decreased when combined with Calcium carbonate.
extended_description: A reduced bioavailability of oral corticosteroids has been observed following their concomitant administration with oral antacids.[A36445] Although there is little clinical evidence of therapeutic failure of corticosteroid therapy resulting from co-administration of antacids, existing pharmacokinetic data suggests the combination be avoided whenever possible.
management: If co-administration of oral corticosteroids with antacids is required, considering separating their administration by at least 2 hours.

summary: Phenelzine may increase the hypertensive activities of Pseudoephedrine.
extended_description: Monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) impede amphetamine metabolism, leading to potentiated effects of amphetamine and enhanced release of norepinephrine and other monoamines from adrenergic nerve endings. Due to synergistic effects, the concurrent use of these medications may result in serotonin syndrome. This can cause headaches, fever, hypertensive crisis, as well as arrhythmias.
management: The use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors and amphetamines concurrently is contraindicated. Avoid the use of amphetamines for 14 days following the administration of monoamine oxidase inhibitors.

summary: The serum concentration of Pseudoephedrine can be increased when it is combined with Calcium carbonate.
extended_description: Antacids may increase the absorption of amphetamines due to the alkalinization of urine, which may result in an increased blood concentration of amphetamines and therefore an increased propensity for adverse effects. This can cause restlessness, tremor, rapid breathing, confusion, panic, aggressiveness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, an arrhythmia, hypertension, and seizures.[A34184,L3309,F342]
management: This drug combination should be avoided, according to product labeling.[F342]

summary: The risk or severity of QTc prolongation can be increased when Salbutamol is combined with Chlorpheniramine.
extended_description: The subject drug may prolong the QTc interval. The affected drug is known to have a moderate risk of prolonging the QTc interval. Concomitant administration of multiple medications that may prolong the QTc interval is a significant risk factor for the development of torsades de pointes (TdP), a potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmia that can arise secondary to QTc prolongation.[A35959,A36748] Other risk factors for the development of TdP include female sex, advanced age, low electrolyte concentrations (e.g. hypokalemia), concomitant diuretic use, bradycardia, and baseline cardiovascular disease.[A35959] There are discrepancies in regards to how QTc interval prolongation should be defined, but a commonly accepted definition is an absolute QTc value of ≥470ms in males and ≥480ms in females.[A187238]
management: The concomitant use of multiple QTc-prolonging agents should be undertaken with care. If co-administration of multiple QTc-prolonging agents is unavoidable, the decision to initiate therapy with these agents should take into consideration the specific medications involved as well as the patient-specific factors listed above. Clinicians should consider the use of a QT-interval risk score[A187238] that allows for assessment of an individual patient's QTc-prolongation risk prior to initiating therapy. Patients requiring therapy with multiple QTc-prolonging medications, particularly those with a higher baseline risk due to non-drug factors, should undergo an ECG assessment of their baseline QTc interval, as well as regular ECGs throughout therapy or as clinically indicated.

summary: Phenelzine may increase the hypoglycemic activities of Tirzepatide.
extended_description: Cases of hypoglycaemia were reported with monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI) therapy [A36707]. Since this condition is a frequently-experienced adverse event of commonly prescribed blood glucose-lowering drugs, co-administration of two agent may lead to increased risk for developing hypoglycaemia.
management: Caution is advised when co-administering a MAOI with blood glucose-lowering agents, particularly in patients at higher risk for developing hypoglycaemia.

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Ramipril is combined with Morphine.
extended_description: Co-administration of agents that are both associated with a risk for developing hypotension, including cases of severe hypotension, may create an additive hypotensive effect to prolong and intensify hypotensive effects.
management: Caution is advised when co-administering two agents with potential to cause hypotension.

summary: The metabolism of Escitalopram can be decreased when combined with Clarithromycin.
extended_description: The subject drug is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor, and the affected drug is metabolized by CYP3A4. Concomitant administration will decrease the metabolism of the affected drug, increasing serum concentrations, as well as the risk and severity of adverse effects.[A15702,A33234,A38501]
management: The co-administration of these drugs should be avoided, or undertaken with extreme caution when necessary. If no alternatives can be found, adjust doses according to instructions on the label and monitor the patient carefully.

summary: The risk or severity of QTc prolongation can be increased when Salbutamol is combined with Escitalopram.
extended_description: The subject drug may prolong the QTc interval. The affected drug has a high risk of prolonging the QTc interval. Concomitant administration of multiple medications that may prolong the QTc interval is a significant risk factor for the development of torsades de pointes (TdP), a potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmia that can arise secondary to QTc prolongation.[A35959,A36748] Other risk factors for the development of TdP include female sex, advanced age, low electrolyte concentrations (e.g. hypokalemia), concomitant diuretic use, bradycardia, and baseline cardiovascular disease.[A35959] There are discrepancies in regards to how QTc interval prolongation should be defined, but a commonly accepted definition is an absolute QTc value of ≥470ms in males and ≥480ms in females.[A187238]
management: The concomitant use of multiple QTc-prolonging agents, particularly agents with a high risk, should be avoided whenever possible. If co-administration of multiple QTc-prolonging agents is unavoidable, the decision to initiate therapy with these agents should take into consideration the specific medications involved as well as the patient-specific factors listed above. Clinicians should consider the use of a QT-interval risk score[A187238] that allows for assessment of an individual patient's QTc-prolongation risk prior to initiating therapy. Patients requiring therapy with multiple QTc-prolonging medications, particularly those with a higher baseline risk due to non-drug factors, should undergo an ECG assessment of their baseline QTc interval, as well as regular ECGs throughout therapy or as clinically indicated.

management: Search for treatment alternatives. If no alternatives can be found, reduce the dose of the affected drug according to the product label and monitor the patient carefully.

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Phenelzine is combined with Chlorpheniramine.
extended_description: Due to their serotonergic actions, uncommon incidences of serotonin syndrome or neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) were rarely reported with the use of serotonin modulators. Combination use of two agents that are associated with the same adverse reaction, such as serotonin syndrome or neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), may result in an additive effect to create prolonged, or intensified adverse reaction.
management: The concomitant use of a serotonin modulator with another serotonin modulator should be undertaken with extreme caution.

summary: The risk or severity of angioedema can be increased when Sitagliptin is combined with Ramipril.
extended_description: Dipeptidyl peptidase-IV (DPP-IV) inhibitors decrease degradation of vasoactive peptides, such as substance P, that may play a role in generating ACE inhibitor-associated angioedema. Co-administration of DPP-IV inhibitors with ACE inhibitors may lead to increased risk for angioedema.[A31721]
management: Concomitant use of DPP-IV inhibitors with ACE inhibitors should be undertaken with caution. Patients should be closely monitored for signs and symptoms of angioedema.

summary: The risk or severity of CNS depression can be increased when Cetirizine is combined with Chlorpheniramine.
extended_description: Central nervous system (CNS) depressants can cause sedation, falls, respiratory depression, coma, and death.[A258998, A259003] The potential interaction between a CNS depressant and another CNS depressant drug due to synergistic effects is well documented in the literature,[A259003, A173965, A259008, A259013] although the risk and severity of CNS depression vary from each drug. The subject and affected drugs are both CNS depressants that, when co-administered, may result in a more profound CNS depression. As the risk and severity of CNS depression resulting from the combined use of CNS depressants vary from each agent, each interaction between CNS depressants should be considered individually.
management: The concomitant use of a CNS depressant with another CNS depressant is generally not recommended. Refer to the individual drug's prescribing information for guidance on dosing and monitoring.

summary: Calcium carbonate can cause a decrease in the absorption of Ferric sulfate resulting in a reduced serum concentration and potentially a decrease in efficacy.
extended_description: An acidic gastric pH appears crucial in both the absorption of iron and the release of iron from dietary and supplementary sources.[A187331] Medications that reduce gastric acidity (i.e. increase gastric pH), such as the subject drug, can therefore impede the absorption (and consequently the efficacy) of orally administered iron supplements. This interaction has been observed primarily with the concomitant use of oral iron supplements and proton pump inhibitors, such as omeprazole, but theoretically may be associated with any agent capable of raising gastric pH.[A35208,A35209,A35210]
management: Patients requiring concomitant oral iron therapy and gastric-acid lowering therapy may require higher doses of elemental iron (or a longer duration of therapy) in order to achieve a therapeutically successful restoration of iron stores.[A35210]

This interaction has been observed primarily with the concomitant use of oral iron supplements and proton pump inhibitors, such as omeprazole, but theoretically may be associated with any agent capable of raising gastric pH.[A35208,A35209,A35210]
management: Patients requiring concomitant oral iron therapy and gastric-acid lowering therapy may require higher doses of elemental iron (or a longer duration of therapy) in order to achieve a therapeutically successful restoration of iron stores.[A35210]

summary: The risk or severity of hypokalemia can be increased when Salbutamol is combined with Furosemide.
extended_description: Beta-adrenergic agonists, such as salmeterol and salbutamol, can cause clinically significant (though generally transient) hypokalemia by shifting potassium from the serum into cells.[L9266,L9428] The concomitant use of these agents with other medications that can lower serum potassium levels, such as loop diuretics, further increases the risk of hypokalemia.[L4332,L9266,L9428]
management: Patients requiring co-administration of beta-adrenergic agonists with other potassium-lowering therapies should be monitored closely for evidence of hypokalemia. As the hypokalemia induced by inhaled beta-agonists is generally transient, occasional use of beta-agonist rescue inhalers is unlikely to pose a significant risk - daily use of beta-agonist therapy, however, may require closer monitoring. If persistent or severe hypokalemia is observed, consider the use of potassium-sparing diuretics (e.g. spironolactone) in place of potassium-wasting diuretics (e.g. furosemide) and/or regular potassium supplementation.

summary: The risk or severity of hypokalemia can be increased when Budesonide is combined with Furosemide.
extended_description: Systemic corticosteroids have the potential to induce hypokalemia.[A34911] The co-administration of corticosteroids with other agents known to lower serum potassium, such as loop diuretics, may lead to an additive risk of developing hypokalemia.[L4332,L9425]
management: Patients requiring concomitant therapy with corticosteroids and other potassium wasting medications should receive regular bloodwork to ensure maintenance of therapeutically appropriate serum potassium concentrations. Supplemental potassium may be required depending on the extent of the hypokalemia.

summary: The risk or severity of gastrointestinal irritation can be increased when Budesonide is combined with Acetylsalicylic acid.
extended_description: Both corticosteroids[A187000] and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)[A187036] are known to cause gastrointestinal irritation, such as ulceration, bleeding, and dyspepsia. Corticosteroids accomplish this via an increase in gastric acidity and a reduction in gastric mucous,[A187000] whereas the mechanism through which NSAIDs contribute to gastrointestinal irritation is thought to be more multifactorial - the acidity of the drug itself likely plays a role in mucosal damage while the systemic COX-inhibitory effects decrease synthesis of gastroprotective prostaglandins.[A187036]
management: Gastrointestinal symptoms such as dyspepsia may be treated symptomatically. If evidence of ulceration or gastrointestinal bleeding is observed, therapy should be discontinued to prevent worsening of symptoms.

summary: Olanzapine may increase the orthostatic hypotensive, hypotensive, and antihypertensive activities of Quetiapine.
extended_description: Since a primary action of adrenergic alpha-1 receptor antagonists is to cause arteries and veins to dilate as a means to treat hypertension, the concomitant use of more than one adrenergic alpha-1 receptor antagonist concurrently would enhance antihypertensive effects and lead to dangerous hypotension or syncopal episodes.
management: The concomitant use of more than one adrenergic alpha-1 receptor antagonists is a contraindication.

summary: The metabolism of Montelukast can be decreased when combined with Trimethoprim.
extended_description: CYP2C8 mediated metabolism is at least one of the avenues by which CYP2C8 substrates are biotransformed and eliminated from the body. When such a substrate is administered concurrently with a moderate CYP2C8 inhibitor, the CYP2C8 mediated metabolism of the substrate will subsequently be reduced, potentially resulting in moderate but noticeable increases in the serum concentration. Such increased serum concentrations may also result in the increased risk, incidence, and/or severity of adverse effects associated with exposure to the given substrate as well.
management: The inhibition of the CYP2C8 mediated metabolism of a CYP2C8 substrate administered concurrently with a moderate CYP2C8 inhibitor may potentially cause a moderate increase in the substrate's serum concentration and/or risk, incidence, or severity of adverse effects. Such an increase can typically be managed via the laboratory monitoring of serum concentrations and appropriate dosage adjustments when necessary or with the use of appropriate alternative therapeutic agents that do not possess the same interaction. Consult the manufacturer labeling specific to individual medications for detailed information about their appropriate use.

Such an increase can typically be managed via the laboratory monitoring of serum concentrations and appropriate dosage adjustments when necessary or with the use of appropriate alternative therapeutic agents that do not possess the same interaction. Consult the manufacturer labeling specific to individual medications for detailed information about their appropriate use.

summary: Olanzapine can cause a decrease in the absorption of Ferric sulfate resulting in a reduced serum concentration and potentially a decrease in efficacy.
extended_description: Absorption of iron from iron salts is pH dependent, decreasing as pH increases [A35739, A35740, A35741]. Histamine H2 receptor antagonists (H2RAs) reduce the secretion of gastric acid which raises the pH in the stomach and intestine. Therefore concomitant use of H2RAs can reduce the absorption of iron from iron salts. While the combination of H2RAs and iron salts has not been investigated, it has been found that acid reduction leads to a decrease in iron absorption [A35739, A35741].
management: Monitor for reduced efficacy of iron salt supplementation when the patient is using H2RAs concomitantly.

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Codeine is combined with Phenelzine.
extended_description: Prescribing information for several different narcotics[L9245,L3699,L9254,L9257] and inhibitors of monoamine oxidase (MAO)[L9248,L9263] recommend against their concurrent use. With some narcotics, such as fentanyl, tramadol, and meperidine, concomitant use with MAO inhibitors can lead to the development of serotonin syndrome.[A31300] The combination of MAO inhibitors with other narcotics, such as morphine, may result in a potentiation of adverse effects such as respiratory depression.[L9245]
management: The concurrent use of narcotics with MAO inhibitors, or the use of narcotics within 14 days of MAO inhibitor use, should be avoided whenever possible. If concomitant therapy is unavoidable, patients should be monitored closely for signs/symptoms suggestive of developing serotonin syndrome or respiratory depression. The narcotic medication should be withdrawn and discontinued immediately if serotonin syndrome is suspected.

summary: The metabolism of Rosuvastatin can be decreased when combined with Clarithromycin.
extended_description: Clarithromycin inhibits the enzymatic activity of CYP3A4 which plays a role in the metabolism of statins that are CYP3A4 substrates. Co-administration of clarithromycin with statins that are primarily metabolized by CYP3A4, especially simvastatin and lovastatin, may lead to increased serum concentrations of those drugs and increased risk for statin-related adverse reactions, including myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.[L8198] Clarithromycin has also been reported to cause rhabdomyolysis in the absence of concomitant statin therapy,[A34471,A34472] which may further increase the risk of myopathy when it is coadministered with statins.
management: The coadministration of clarithromycin with statins that are extensively metabolized by CYP3A4, namely simvastatin and lovastatin, is contraindicated.[L8198] Concurrent use with other statins should be undertaken with caution, and patients should be cautioned to monitor closely for signs or symptoms of developing myopathy. If short-term therapy with clarithromycin is required, patients may be instructed to hold the administration of their statin for the duration of antibiotic therapy[A34481] - as the therapeutic efficacy of statins requires long-term use in order to lower plasma lipid levels, withdrawal of therapy for 7-14 days (i.e. the duration of antibiotic therapy) is unlikely to cause any significant changes in its effect.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Citalopram can be increased when used in combination with Pregabalin.
extended_description: Pregabalin may potentiate the effects of central nervous system depressants due to additive effects. In post-marketing experience, there are reports of respiratory failure and coma in patients taking pregabalin alone or in combination with other CNS depressants.
management: Inform patients who require concomitant treatment with central nervous system depressants such as opiates or benzodiazepines that they may experience additive CNS side effects, such as somnolence. Consider administering lower doses of CNS depressants and/or pregabalin. Monitor for respiratory depression. Advise patients to avoid the use of alcohol while taking pregabalin, due to additive effects.

summary: The risk or severity of renal failure, hypotension, and hyperkalemia can be increased when Ramipril is combined with Losartan.
extended_description: The use of multiple agents affecting the renin-angiotensin system can result in additive toxicity due to overlapping adverse effect profiles.[A33935,A33936] These synergistic adverse effects are likely to be consistent with the adverse effect profile of the individual drugs implicated in this interaction, and are likely to include an increased incidence and/or severity of hypotension, hyperkalemia, and acute renal failure.[L7456,L7423]
management: Patients requiring concomitant therapy with multiple agents acting on the renin-angiotensin system should be monitored throughout therapy for evidence of hypotension, hyperkalemia, and renal failure. Regular lab work to assess serum electrolyte and renal status should be conducted as indicated.

summary: Itraconazole may decrease the excretion rate of Nitrofurantoin which could result in a higher serum level.
extended_description: Breast Cancer Resistance Protein is a ATP-binding cassette (ABC) efflux transporter that limits intestinal absorption of low-permeability substrate drugs and mediates biliary excretion of drugs and metabolites [A37243]. Pharmacokinetic interaction may occur from co-administration of drugs that are eliminated by BCRP with BCRP inhibitors that attenuate the activity of the transporter. Increased serum concentrations of and systemic exposure to BCRP substrates may result in altered therapeutic efficacy of the drug therapy and increase the risk for developing drug-related adverse reactions.
management: Caution is advised when co-administering BCRP inhibitors with BCRP substrates, especially sensitive substrates that predominantly depend on BCRPs for drug elimination. Consider choosing alternate drug options that do not involve the inhibition of BCRP.

Increased serum concentrations of and systemic exposure to BCRP substrates may result in altered therapeutic efficacy of the drug therapy and increase the risk for developing drug-related adverse reactions.
management: Caution is advised when co-administering BCRP inhibitors with BCRP substrates, especially sensitive substrates that predominantly depend on BCRPs for drug elimination. Consider choosing alternate drug options that do not involve the inhibition of BCRP.

summary: The serum concentration of Fexofenadine can be increased when it is combined with Simvastatin.
extended_description: This interaction may lead to increased exposure to drugs which are p-glycoprotein substrates, increasing their risk of toxicity. Central nervous system depression, undertreated HIV infection, cardiac conduction defects, and transplant rejection are all possible outcomes if these interactions occur [F1133].
management: Due to the risk of toxicity, p-glycoprotein substrates and inhibitors coadministration should be exercised with caution. These combinations should be considered on a case by case basis depending on labeling instructions for specific drugs and the degree of p-glycoprotein inhibition.

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Naproxen is combined with Ibuprofen.
extended_description: Combining multiple non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents has the potential to increase the risk of adverse effects including GI bleeding, kidney dysfunction, and non-fatal myocardial infarction with no proven benefit over single agent therapy.[A33567,A33573,A33574] The only exception to this interaction is low dose acetylsalicylic acid for cardioprotection.[L12693]
management: Avoid the use of multiple non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents.[L12693] Combine with acetaminophen or other analgesics from different classes if greater pain relief is needed. This is less of a concern if systemic non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents are combined with topical agents. Topical use should be limited to one non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agent per location due to a lack of evidence for additional benefit from a second agent.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Salbutamol can be decreased when used in combination with Bisoprolol.
extended_description: Due to opposing pharmacological effects, adrenergic beta-antagonists may reduce the efficacy of adrenergic agonists. The cardio-stimulating and bronchodilatory effects of adrenergic agonists may be antagonized by beta-adrenergic blocking drugs, such as propranolol. Anaphylaxis may be worsened by beta blockers because these beta blockers decrease the effectiveness of adrenergic agonists, such as epinephrine. This is especially dangerous when adrenergic agonists are being used for anaphylactic episodes or cardiovascular shock.
management: It is not advisable to coadminister adrenergic agonists and beta antagonists.

summary: The risk or severity of hemorrhage can be increased when Fluoxetine is combined with Apixaban.
extended_description: It has been reported that concomitant administration of antiplatelet agents and SSRI is associated with an increase in hemorrhage. This interaction is due to the inhibition of serotonin reuptake in platelets which produces a reduction of serotonin to even 1% of the normal quantity. Serotonin is very important for the aggregation of platelets and the lack of serotonin does not allow the normal aggregation process of the platelets.
management: Monitor INR regularly when these agents are given concomitantly. If major changes are observed, change or suspend serotonergic treatment.

summary: The risk or severity of dehydration can be increased when Bendroflumethiazide is combined with Lactulose.
extended_description: Both laxatives and diuretics can lead to dehydration due to their pharmacological action. Although laxatives have various mechanisms of action, the overall effects tend to be fluid retention in stools for softer stool consistency and thus easier bowel movements.[A259477, A259482, A259487,A259492,A259497,A34399,A259502] On the other hand, diuretics increase urine output by the kidney through inhibition of water reabsorption, thus leading to hypovolemia and eventually dehydration. Therefore, the co-administration of diuretics and laxatives can have an additive effect on diuresis and worsen dehydration.[A259517]
management: Avoid this combination when possible. If required, closely monitor fluid status and electrolytes and adjust dosages of laxatives and/or diuretics as necessary.

summary: The risk or severity of dehydration can be increased when Furosemide is combined with Lactulose.
extended_description: Both laxatives and diuretics can lead to dehydration due to their pharmacological action. summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Lactulose can be decreased when used in combination with Amlodipine.
extended_description: Calcium channel blockers as a class have a tendency to cause constipation.[F591,L1485,L3569] Due to opposing pharmacological effects, then, concomitant administration of calcium channel blockers with laxatives may render the latter less effective.
management: Dose changes are generally unnecessary - patients requiring laxatives who are already receiving therapy with a calcium channel blocker should be made aware that laxative efficacy may be impaired. Trials of different laxatives or even combinations of multiple laxatives may be required in order to attain the desired therapeutic effect.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Lactulose can be decreased when used in combination with Morphine.
extended_description: Opioids may decrease the effects of laxatives as their effect on the GI tract produces a reduction in gastric motility, counteracting the effect of the laxative.
management: Adjust dosage or discontinue one treatment. Monitor patient response and search for opioid alternatives.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Lactulose can be decreased when used in combination with Codeine.
extended_description: Opioids may decrease the effects of laxatives as their effect on the GI tract produces a reduction in gastric motility, counteracting the effect of the laxative.
management: Adjust dosage or discontinue one treatment. Monitor patient response and search for opioid alternatives.

summary: The serum concentration of Atorvastatin can be increased when it is combined with Itraconazole.
extended_description: Itraconazole is a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4 that may attenuate the metabolism of drug predominantly metabolized by CYP3A4, such as atorvastatin. Based on pharmacokinetic data, concomitant administration of atorvastatin (20 to 40 mg) and itraconazole (200 mg) was associated with a 2.5-3.3-fold increase in atorvastatin AUC. Increased serum concentrations of atorvastatin and systemic exposure may lead to increased risk for statin-related muscle toxicity.
management: Co-administration of atorvastatin with atorvastatin is not recommended; if concomitant use is clinically unavoidable, the dose of atorvastatin should be reduced accordingly.

summary: The serum concentration of Simvastatin can be increased when it is combined with Itraconazole.
extended_description: Metabolism and elimination of simvastatin primarily depends on CYP3A4-mediated pathways and may be significantly impaired with the presence of a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor, itraconazole. Co-administration of the drug may lead to increased serum concentrations of the drug and increased risk for statin-related adverse reactions, such as myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
management: Co-administration of itraconzaole with simvastatin is contraindicated. If treatment with itraconazole is unavoidable, therapy with simvastatin must be suspended during the course of treatment.

summary: The metabolism of Warfarin can be decreased when combined with Atorvastatin.
extended_description: Atorvastatin is an inhibitor of CYP3A4, which may attenuate the metabolism of vitamin K antagonists that are CYP3A4 substrates, such as (R)-warfarin and phenprocoumon. Concomitant use of two agents may lead to increased plasma concentrations of vitamin K antagonists and risk for drug-related adverse reactions such as bleeding.
management: Concomitant use of atorvastatin and vitamin K antagonists/coumarin anticoagulants should be undertaken with caution. A close monitoring of INR and with adjustment of the anticoagulant dose may be necessary [A34264, L3336].

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Dapagliflozin can be increased when used in combination with Rosuvastatin.
extended_description: Sulfonamides increase the hypoglycemic effect of blood glucose lowering agents which may reduce blood sugar levels and produce an intensified effect.[A34822,A36662] The sulfonamides can reduce blood sugar by about 20% on their own.[A34822]
management: Monitor patient response and suspend treatment if necessary.[A34822,A34856,A36662]

summary: The serum concentration of Metformin can be increased when it is combined with Clopidogrel.
extended_description: Organic cation transporter 2 (OCT2) is a renal transporter that is expressed in the basolateral and apical membrane of renal proximal tubules to promote tubular secretion and reabsorption of drug molecules.[A35226,L1193] The subject drug is an OCT2 inhibitor and the affected drug is an OCT2 substrate. Co-administration of these drugs may increase serum concentrations of OCT2 substrates, increasing the risk of adverse effects.[A35719,A35720]
management: Use caution when administering OCT2 inhibitors with OCT2 substrates as the severity of this interaction is variable.[A35719,A35720] If these drugs must be coadministered, monitor the patient for signs of toxicity and adjust doses according to product labels.[A35719]

summary: Calcium carbonate can cause a decrease in the absorption of Acetaminophen resulting in a reduced serum concentration and potentially a decrease in efficacy.
extended_description: Antacids can significantly reduce the rate of absorption of acetaminophen.[A36368,A186155] This effect is suggested to be caused by the adsorption of acetaminophen to the antacid.[A36368]
management: The clinical relevance of this interaction is likely negligible[A186155] as it is not listed in the FDA label.[L4396]

summary: The risk or severity of QTc prolongation can be increased when Pregabalin is combined with Salbutamol.
extended_description: Both the subject and affected drug have the potential to cause prolongation of the cardiac QTc interval. Concurrent use of multiple QTc-prolonging medications may result in an additive effect on the QTc interval, enhancing prolongation and increasing the risk of sudden cardiac death due to Torsades de Pointes (TdP), a type of ventricular tachycardia.[A35959] The risk of developing TdP is also increased by a number of patient-specific factors, such as advanced age, female gender, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, and concomitant diuretic use, amongst others.[A35959,A36751] There are discrepancies in regards to how QTc interval prolongation should be defined, but a commonly accepted definition is an absolute QTc value of ≥470ms in males and ≥480ms in females.[A187238]
management: As the severity of this interaction will vary from patient to patient, the decision to initiate therapy with multiple potential QTc-prolonging medications should take into consideration the specific medications involved as well as the patient-specific factors listed above. Clinicians should consider the use of a QT-interval risk score [A187238] that allows for assessment of an individual patient's QTc-prolongation risk prior to initiating therapy. Patients requiring therapy with multiple QTc-prolonging medications, particularly those with a higher baseline risk due to non-drug factors, should undergo an ECG assessment of their baseline QTc interval, as well as regular ECGs throughout therapy or as clinically indicated.

extended_description: Co-administration OATP1B1 substrates with inhibitors of that transporter may lead to increased serum concentrations of the substrates due to attenuated transporter-mediated efflux. management: Caution is advised when co-administering OATP1B1 substrates and inhibitors. Monitor therapy.

summary: Flucloxacillin may decrease the excretion rate of Nitrofurantoin which could result in a higher serum level.
extended_description: Bile salt export pump (BSEP/ABCB11) plays a crucial role in the secretion of bile acids from the liver.[A16121,A187126,A36983] For this reason, the concurrent administration of two or more bile salt export pump (BSEP/ABCB11) substrates will compete for the BSEP transporter with bile acids, leading to hepatotoxicity from the accumulation of bile acids or adverse effects from the accumulation of the substrate drugs.

summary: Folic acid may decrease the excretion rate of Trimethoprim which could result in a higher serum level.
extended_description: The renal excretion of drugs is the overall result of a combination of kidney processes that include glomerular filtration, passive diffusion, tubular secretion, and tubular reabsorption.[A38743] Since two of these mechanisms - tubular secretion and reabsorption - are saturable processes [A38743], they are consequently susceptible to competition between multiple substrates excreted by the kidneys. If two or more medications that are mainly renally excreted are co-administered, they may compete for renal elimination; there is a large likelihood that one agent may "out-compete" or saturate the renal excretion mechanisms before the other concomitantly administered agent(s) are excreted. As a result, the elimination of these other concurrently administered agents may be inhibited or otherwise delayed, which could lead to increases in their serum concentrations and the risk, incidence, and/or severity of adverse effects associated with the exposure to such drugs.[A37360, A37361, A35970, A38743]
management: Refer to individual drug prescribing information for dosing or management guidelines. Caution is generally advised.

If two or more medications that are mainly renally excreted are co-administered, they may compete for renal elimination; there is a large likelihood that one agent may "out-compete" or saturate the renal excretion mechanisms before the other concomitantly administered agent(s) are excreted. As a result, the elimination of these other concurrently administered agents may be inhibited or otherwise delayed, which could lead to increases in their serum concentrations and the risk, incidence, and/or severity of adverse effects associated with the exposure to such drugs.[A37360, A37361, A35970, A38743]
management: Refer to individual drug prescribing information for dosing or management guidelines. Caution is generally advised.

summary: Furosemide may decrease the excretion rate of Warfarin which could result in a higher serum level.
extended_description: The subject drug impairs renal function, and the affected drug is mainly excreted by the kidneys.[A35970] As dosing of drugs that are mainly renally excreted is heavily affected by renal impairment, this drug interaction can lead to a decrease in drug elimination via damage to the kidneys.[L46521] Increased exposure to the affected narrow therapeutic index drug is hazardous. Some exceptions may exist and require close clinical monitoring.
management: Avoid concomitant use of these agents, due to the risk of renal toxicity and reduced elimination. Certain exceptions may exist, but likely require close monitoring of drug levels or clinical signs and symptoms. If this combination cannot be avoided, consider changing the dose of either drug. Nephrotoxic drugs cause various clinical syndromes, including renal failure, proteinuria, and tubulopathy. Decreases in creatinine clearance also occur. Monitor for the above signs and adjust therapy accordingly. Refer to product monographs and guidance related to dosing and monitoring.

summary: Pregabalin may decrease the excretion rate of Warfarin which could result in a higher serum level.
extended_description: The renal excretion of drugs is the overall result of a combination of kidney processes that include glomerular filtration, passive diffusion, tubular secretion, and tubular reabsorption.[A38743] Since two of these mechanisms - tubular secretion and reabsorption - are saturable processes [A38743], they are consequently susceptible to competition between multiple substrates excreted by the kidneys. summary: Bendroflumethiazide may increase the excretion rate of Folic acid which could result in a lower serum level and potentially a reduction in efficacy.
extended_description: The subject drug induces diuresis[A37446,A37447 ], which can theoretically increase the excretion rate of the affected drug, which is eliminated by the kidneys. Additionally, it could affect renal tubular reabsorption of certain drugs. Exposure to the affected drug can be markedly reduced, leading to subtherapeutic drug levels that are unlikely to elicit an adequate clinical response.
management: Monitor the patient when these drugs are administered concomitantly, and adjust the dose as necessary. Suspend the use of the diuretic if the efficacy of the affected drug is significantly decreased or negatively affects the patient.

summary: The risk or severity of methemoglobinemia can be increased when Acetaminophen is combined with Nitrofurantoin.
extended_description: Methemoglobinemia, condition characterized by increased quantities of hemoglobin in which the iron of heme is oxidized to the ferric (Fe3+) form [A37584], can occur either in congenital or acquired forms and is often manifested with grey cyanosis. It is also caused from exposure to drugs that directly oxidize hemoglobin to methemoglobin [A37584] or those that require metabolic activation to an oxidising species [A1745]. The risk for developing drug-induced methemoglobinemia may be elevated with the concomitant use of other agents known to cause the same adverse event due to an additive effect, and the duration or intensity of methemoglobinemia may be worsened. This interaction may be more profound in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency or congenital or idiopathic methemoglobinemia. Young infants under the age of 12 months may be particularly susceptible to this complication because of their low stomach acid production, large number of nitrite-reducing bacteria, and the relatively easy oxidation of fetal hemoglobin [A37584]. Small infants have lower erythrocyte levels of cytochrome b reductase [A37584].
management: Caution should be exercised when methemoglobinemia-associated agent is used in conjunction with other agents with potential to cause methemoglobinemia. If methemoglobinemia develops, the use of these agents should be discontinued and appropriate medical intervention should be initiated. Such combination use should be avoided in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency or congenital or idiopathic methemoglobinemia, or in infants under the age of 12 months.

summary: The risk or severity of bleeding and hemorrhage can be increased when Phenelzine is combined with Clopidogrel.
extended_description: It has been reported that concomitant administration of antiplatelet agents and monoamine oxidase inhibitor antidepressants that increase the levels of serotonin are associated with an increase in hemorrhage. This interaction is due to the inhibition of serotonin reuptake in platelets which produces a reduction of serotonin to even 1% of the normal quantity. Serotonin is very important for the aggregation of platelets and the lack of serotonin does not allow the normal aggregation process of the platelets.
management: Monitor INR regularly when these agents are given concomitantly. If major changes are observed, change or suspend serotonergic treatment.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Lactulose can be decreased when used in combination with Fluoxetine.
extended_description: Constipation as an adverse effect associated with the use of anticholinergic agents has been well documented [A37908]. Subsequently, there is a concern that the concomitant use of an anticholinergic agent and a laxative agent(s) could interfere with or otherwise decrease the therapeutic action of the laxative due to the constipation adverse effect of the anticholinergic medication. Nevertheless, there have also been studies suggesting that while anticholinergics have been associated with constipation, medications with marked anticholinergic effects were not particularly associated with the effectiveness of laxatives [A37910, A37911].
management: Individuals who are concomitantly using anticholinergic agents with laxatives should be counseled to be aware that constipation is a relatively common adverse effect associated with anticholinergic use and that there is a possibility that it could interfere with the action of the laxative.

summary: The risk or severity of myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and myoglobinuria can be increased when Trimethoprim is combined with Rosuvastatin.
extended_description: Muscle toxicity, including myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and myoglobinuria, may be caused by a variety of pharmacological agents. The major symptoms in drug-induced myopathies are proximal muscle weakness, increased muscle enzyme levels, electromyographic changes and histological lesions [A34627,A38135]. Toxic effects on the muscular system may be additive or synergistic when two agents with the potential to cause muscle toxicity are used concurrently.[A259447]
management: The concurrent use of medications known to cause myopathy and rhabdomyolysis should be generally avoided, if possible.[A259447] Monitor for signs of muscle toxicity when administering agents that are associated with muscle toxicity, especially when more than one such agent is administered.

summary: The risk or severity of angioedema can be increased when Ramipril is combined with Pregabalin.
extended_description: Angioedema, characterized by deep dermal, subcutaneous and/or mucosal swelling, can be cause by drugs via various mechanisms. It may occur in the the face, lips, and tongue, and may potentially lead to a life-threatening condition in cases where the pharynx or larynx is involved [A38153]. Concomitant use of two agents associated with a risk for developing angioedema may result in an additive effect to further elevated the risk for experiencing angioedema.
management: Caution is advised when co-administering two agents with potential to cause drug-induced angioedema. Closely monitor patients for signs and symptoms of angioedema.

summary: The metabolism of Atorvastatin can be decreased when combined with Amitriptyline.
extended_description: Uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase 1A1 (UGT1A1) is a conjugating enzyme that facilitates the metabolism of various drugs by transferring one glucuronic acid to the molecule, making them more hydrophilic, and more favourable for biliary or renal elimination [A34299]. Inhibitors of this enzyme may attenuate the metabolism of various drugs that depend on this enzyme-mediated clearance thus increase the plasma concentrations of these drugs. Increased systemic exposure to the UGT1A1 substrates may result in altered clinical efficacy and increased risk for developing drug-related adverse events.
management: The concomitant use of UGT1A1 substrates with UGT1A1 inducers should be generally avoided.

summary: The metabolism of Methotrexate can be increased when combined with Warfarin.
extended_description: The subject drug is a weak CYP3A4 enzyme inducer, and the affected drug is metabolized by the CYP3A4 enzyme. Concomitant administration of these agents will induce the metabolism of the CYP3A4 substrate (affected drug), reducing the serum concentration and therapeutic effect. Drugs with a narrow therapeutic index must be maintained within a specific concentration range in order to be safe and efficacious. Reduced concentration of a drug with a narrow therapeutic index may lead to significantly lower efficacy.
management: It is not advisable to administer these drugs concurrently.

summary: The metabolism of Lansoprazole can be decreased when combined with Simvastatin.
extended_description: CYP2C8 mediated metabolism is at least one of the avenues by which CYP2C8 substrates are biotransformed and eliminated from the body. When such a substrate is administered concurrently with a CYP2C8 inhibitor of unknown strength, the CYP2C8 mediated metabolism of the substrate will subsequently be reduced - either slightly, moderately, or greatly. Such decreases in metabolism consequently result in increases in the serum concentration and the risk, incidence, and/or severity of adverse effects associated with exposure to the given substrate as well.
management: The inhibition of the CYP2C8 mediated metabolism of a CYP2C8 substrate administered concurrently with a CYP2C8 inhibitor of unknown strength may or may not be so great that the resultant increase in the substrate's serum concentration and/or risk, incidence, or severity of adverse effects may be significant enough to have the interaction considered a contraindication. Laboratory monitoring of the serum concentrations of the agents involved and observation of signs and symptoms of adverse effects and/or toxicity in patients is essential to determining how the CYP2C8 inhibitor's unknown potency may be managed. The use of two such agents together is generally cautioned against and the use of alternative, non-interacting therapeutic agents is strongly recommended. Only if the combination therapy is absolutely required and appropriate, dosage adjustments of one or both agents based upon the monitoring of patients' clinical response to the therapy should be made as necessary. Consult the manufacturer labeling specific to individual medications for detailed information about their appropriate use.

summary: The metabolism of Sitagliptin can be decreased when combined with Naproxen.
extended_description: CYP2C8 mediated metabolism is at least one of the avenues by which CYP2C8 substrates are biotransformed and eliminated from the body. Consequently, when a CYP2C8 substrate is coadministered with another CYP2C8 substrate, both substrates will invariably compete with each other to be metabolized by the limited quantities of CYP2C8 isoenzymes present in the body. When one substrate is subsequently capable of 'out-competing' the other, the other substrate will have its CYP2C8 facilitated metabolism stalled or otherwise decreased for a time, resulting in increased serum concentrations of the substrate and/or an increased risk, incidence, or severity of adverse effects associated with exposure to the substrate. The outcomes of the interaction of different CYP2C8 substrates depend of the dosage, timing of administration., and the degree of metabolism by CYP2C8 for each substrate.
management: Depending on the extent to which the metabolism of one of the coadministered CYP2C8 substrates is decreased, the resultant increase in that substrate's serum concentration and/or risk, incidence, or severity of adverse effects may be significant enough to have the interaction considered a contraindication. Laboratory monitoring of the serum concentrations of the agents involved and observation of signs and symptoms of adverse effects and/or toxicity in patients is essential to determining how the competition between the two substrates for the same CYP2C8 mediated metabolism is affecting the medications. The use of two such agents together is generally cautioned against and the use of alternative, non-interacting therapeutic agents is strongly recommended. Only if the combination therapy is absolutely required and appropriate, dosage adjustments of one or both agents based upon the monitoring of patients' clinical response to the therapy should be made as necessary. Consult the manufacturer labeling specific to individual medications for detailed information about their appropriate use. For products with a combination of both CYP2C8 substrates, monitor for serum concentrations of individual substrate and signs of adverse effects.

Only if the combination therapy is absolutely required and appropriate, dosage adjustments of one or both agents based upon the monitoring of patients' clinical response to the therapy should be made as necessary. For products with a combination of both CYP2C8 substrates, monitor for serum concentrations of individual substrate and signs of adverse effects.

summary: The metabolism of Montelukast can be decreased when combined with Acetaminophen.
extended_description: When a CYP2A6 substrate is coadministered with another CYP2A6 substrate, both substrates will invariably compete with each other to be metabolized by the limited quantities of CYP2A6 isoenzymes present in the body. summary: The risk or severity of bleeding and gastrointestinal bleeding can be increased when Ibuprofen is combined with Apixaban.
extended_description: When ibuprofen and apixaban are coadministered, there may be an increased risk of bleeding, including serious and fatal gastrointestinal bleeding. This occurs due to the additive anticoagulant effects of both drugs.
management: Avoid this combination when possible. The benefits of coadministration must outweigh the risks. Carefully consider the risks of this combination. If coadministration is necessary, monitor closely for signs of bleeding (including gastrointestinal bleeding). According to the ibuprofen FDA label, most spontaneous reports of fatal gastrointestinal bleeding events are in elderly or debilitated patients. Special care should be taken in treating this population of patients. To minimize the risk for adverse gastrointestinal events in patients treated with an NSAID, the lowest effective dose should be used for the shortest period of time [F1848]. The apixaban FDA label does not state that this combination is contraindicated, however, does suggest an increased risk of bleeding when NSAID drugs such as ibuprofen are coadministered with apixaban [F1847].

summary: The risk or severity of hypoglycemia can be increased when Metformin is combined with Semaglutide.
extended_description: The primary therapeutic effect of blood glucose lowering agents is to decrease blood sugar levels [A39640, A38089]. Consequently, when two or more such agents are used in combination there is a concern that their mutual abilities to decrease blood sugar levels would be enhanced, resulting in the possibility of excessive blood glucose lowering or potentially severe or even fatal hypoglycemia [A39640, A38089]. A number of factors can affect the degree of hypoglycemia, including the specific blood glucose lowering agents combined [A39640, A38089] and the unique characteristics of the patients (age, co-morbidities, other concomitant medication therapies, etc.) to whom the combination therapies are administered. When considering such combinations, it is advised to review the specific prescribing information of the agents involved and the specific blood sugar monitoring and control of the patients treated.
management: Although the combined use of blood glucose agents is often times clinically appropriate, it is necessary to review and monitor the particular blood glucose levels and control of the specific patients being treated [A39640, A38089] so that dose reductions of one or more of the agents being used concomitantly may be considered to reduce the risk of hypoglycemia as per the reaction of the patient(s) [A39640, A38089, F2010].

summary: Ibuprofen may decrease the antiplatelet activities of Acetylsalicylic acid.
extended_description: Acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) reduces risk of myocardial infarction via the inhibition of thromboxane production by platelets, preventing the formation of clots which contribute to atherosclerosis. Certain non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen, may interfere with this inhibitory action, reducing cardioprotective effects. This effect was not observed when diclofenac was used with ASA. [A39639,A39641,A177499,A186313]. Furthermore, the prescribing information for ibuprofen mentions that the protein binding for ibuprofen is decreased when administered with ASA, likely due to competition for binding.[L7402]
management: Concomitant administration of ibuprofen and aspirin is not generally recommended.[L7402] Use diclofenac or other NSAIDS instead of ibuprofen when ASA is administered.[A177499] If this combination is used long term, the patient should be made aware of the possibility of reduced cardioprotective effects.

summary: Metformin may increase the hypolipidaemic activities of Simvastatin.
extended_description: A study in which diabetic rats were treated with metformin & atorvastatin and metformin & rosuvastatin combination therapies demonstrated that these concomitant therapies decreased serum triglyceride, total cholesterol, and low-density lipoprotein levels much more than atorvastatin and rosuvastatin therapies alone [L4720]. Although these results seemingly suggest a pharmacodynamic interaction between metformin and statins like atorvastatin and rosuvastatin to enhance the hypolipidaemic action of statins, the specific mechanism of action has not yet been formally elucidated and the availability of such studies only in animal models suggests dedicated studies are necessary to extend investigations regarding the effects of such combination therapy in humans [A39656].
management: Patients with Type 2 diabetes mellitus frequently take metformin and statin combination therapy for the appropriate management and/or control of blood sugar levels and associated cardiovascular disease risk [A39656]. Despite this interaction not being overly demonstrable in diabetic human patients taking such combination therapy [L4720, A39656], the concomitant use of metformin and statins in diabetic patients still necessitates the regular review and monitoring of patients' specific blood sugar control and lipid profiles as a means of assessing the continued effectiveness of the combined therapy.

summary: The risk or severity of hypoglycemia can be increased when Amlodipine is combined with Insulin glargine.
extended_description: The results of the Reasons for Geographic and Racial Differences in Stroke (REGARDS) study demonstrated that the use of calcium channel blockers, and verapamil in particular, was associated with lower fasting blood glucose levels in patients with diabetes [A39664]. Moreover, serum glucose was substantially lower in diabetic patients who used a combination of calcium channel blockers (including verapamil) and blood glucose lowering agents like oral agents and/or insulin alone [A39664]. In particular, it is believed that calcium channel blockers, and in particular verapamil, are capable of decreasing the expression of thioredoxin interacting protien (TXNIP), which results in a variety of activities such as decreased beta-cell apoptosis, increased beta-cell mass, and enhanced endogenous insulin levels [A39664].
management: Despite the findings in the REGARDS study, this interaction is not necessarily widely observed and additional studies are needed to determine the therapeutic potential of verapamil or other calcium channel blockers in the treatment of severe type 2 diabetes mellitus [A39664]. In addition, despite adjustment for a wide variety of participant characteristics, there is still a possibility of unmeasured confounding factors in the REGARDS study findings, as in all observational settings [A39664].

summary: The absorption of Ferric sulfate can be decreased when combined with Omeprazole.
extended_description: Omeprazole interferes with the gastric pH necessary for the absorption of iron compounds. This may result in decreased efficacy of iron-containing drugs and dietary iron, leading to iron deficiency anemia. Studies regarding this interaction are conflicting, however, it is advisable to exercise caution while these drugs are coadministered [A35209], [A35210], [A35211], [A39688].
management: Avoid this combination when possible and avoid administering omeprazole for long-term therapy while iron is being coadministered. Iron-deficient patients taking proton pump inhibitors may need to be treated with high dose iron therapy for a longer duration or with intravenous iron therapy [A35210]. Monitor CBC regularly and adjust dosage as necessary.

summary: The risk or severity of lactic acidosis can be increased when Ramipril is combined with Metformin.
extended_description: Antihypertensives like angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEI) are capable of acutely impairing the renal function of a patient via reductions in the glomerular filtration rate [A39739, A35178]. Although such a decrease usually stabilizes after a few days of use or reverses upon termination of therapy, the effect can become serious in patients experiencing decreased renal perfusion associated with conditions like renal artery stenosis, heart failure, polycystic kidney disease, or volume depletion [A39739, A35178]. In such conditions, the maintenance of patients' glomerular filtration rates (GFR) relies on angiotensin-II dependent efferent vasomotor tone - a tone that is minimized with the use of ACEI [A39739, A35178]. Moreover, considering that lactic acidosis is an adverse effect commonly associated with exposure to metformin, any possible impairment of renal function due to the concomitant use of ACEI can result in metformin accumulation and ultimately an increased risk of lactic acidosis [L4340].
management: Some medicinal products can adversely affect renal function which may increase the risk of lactic acidosis, like the concomitant use of ACEI with metformin. When starting or using such products in combination with metformin, close monitoring of renal function is necessary [L4340].

summary: The risk or severity of serotonin syndrome can be increased when Pseudoephedrine is combined with Sertraline.
extended_description: These agents have demonstrated a capability to increase the risk of serotonin syndrome in a patient via mechanisms that include causing increases in the body serotonin levels [A31446, F1207, F1209]. As a consequence, the combination use of two or more such agents can result in an additive action that can substantially increase the risk or incidence of serotonin syndrome in a patient who is being administered these medications [A31446, F1207, F1209].
management: The concomitant use of serotonin modulating agents that can increase the risk of serotonin syndrome should be undertaken with extreme caution. Treatment should be discontinued immediately if and when any signs or symptoms of serotonin syndrome first occur.

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Codeine is combined with Cimetidine.
extended_description: Due to a reduction in drug transport, OCT1 inhibitors may increase the concentration of drugs that are substrates of this transporter, leading to OCT1 substrate intolerance.
management: Avoid this combination when possible. Monitor for adverse effects of the OCT1 substrate. Some combinations may be contraindicated. Refer to individual product labeling.

summary: The risk or severity of bleeding can be increased when Lactulose is combined with Warfarin.
extended_description: Due to an unknown mechanism, lactulose may increase INR in patients treated with warfarin, and therefore increase the risk of bleeding.
management: Avoid this combination when possible to prevent serious bleeding. If coadministration is required, closely monitor INR and adjust warfarin dose accordingly.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Bisoprolol can be decreased when used in combination with Calcium carbonate.
extended_description: Calcium may decrease the effects of adrenergic beta-blockers when given concomitantly. There is no conclusive evidence regarding this interaction.[L4785] There have been reports that calcium may decrease the effects of beta blockers.[A39905] Another clinical study determined that calcium prolonged the half-life of atenolol.[A39908] Evidence surrounding this interaction is conflicting and the mechanism of this interaction is unclear.
management: Do not administer excessive calcium in any preparation. Administer atenolol 2 hours before or 6 hours after calcium products.[A39905]

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Metformin can be decreased when used in combination with Levothyroxine.
extended_description: Some case reports and literature suggest that high levels of thyroid hormone cause hyperglycemia due to decreased insulin secretion. This could result in reduced efficacy of drugs intended to control hyperglycemia.[A181550,L4330] Thyroid hormone is a major regulator of metabolism and regulation of energy; elevated thyroid hormone levels can promote gluconeogenesis and peripheral insulin resistance, leading to glucose intolerance.[A181550,A187172,A187175]
management: Caution is advised when co-administering metformin with agents used to treat hypothyroidism. Be cautious not to over treat hypothyroidism. Blood glucose should be monitored at appropriate intervals. When thyroid drugs are withdrawn from a patient on metformin, the patient should be monitored for hypoglycemia.[L4330]

summary: Tiotropium may increase the anticholinergic activities of Loratadine.
extended_description: Loratadine may carry some risk of adverse effects related to its anticholinergic activity (e.g. dry mouth, nasal dryness, etc)[L8486] - its combined use with other anticholinergic medications may, theoretically, result in additive anticholinergic action and enhanced adverse effects. In practice, the clinical relevance of this interaction is unclear. Within the spectrum of oral antihistamines, loratadine's anticholinergic activity is relatively low,[A39957] and prescribing information for loratadine cites animal studies in which very little _in vivo_ anticholinergic activity was observed.[L8486]
management: Patients using multiple concomitant anticholinergic medications should monitor for signs of enhanced anticholinergic activity. Dose adjustments or discontinuation of therapy are unlikely to be required, but may be considered on a case-by-case basis depending on the severity of the anticholinergic effects.

summary: The risk or severity of Tachycardia can be increased when Pseudoephedrine is combined with Tiotropium.
extended_description: These two agents are known to produce tachycardia and thus, concomitant administration is not recommended as the effects may be additive.
management: This combination is not recommended. If absolutely necessary, monitor patients for signs of tachycardia.

summary: Doxycycline can cause a decrease in the absorption of Methotrexate resulting in a reduced serum concentration and potentially a decrease in efficacy.
extended_description: The tetracyclines may competitively displace or inhibit the serum protein binding of methotrexate [F2305], [L4871]. The interaction may also occur by the tetracyclines inhibiting the intestinal absorption of methotrexate [F2304], [A35038]. This drug class may also interfere with the enterohepatic circulation by inhibiting bowel flora and suppressing metabolism of methotrexate by bacteria [F2304].
management: Avoid this combination when possible. The benefits of this combination must outweigh the risks. Administer this combination with caution, and monitor the patient for methotrexate efficacy.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Bisoprolol can be decreased when used in combination with Pseudoephedrine.
extended_description: Due to opposing pharmacologic actions, the efficacy of adrenergic blocking agents, such as the affected drug, may be reduced by co-administration with amphetamines.[L15242,F2358,F2361]
management: Consider the possibility of decreased alpha-adrenergic blocking activity if choosing to co-administer amphetamines with alpha-blocking agents. Patients may require dose adjustments to one (or both) medications(s) to account for altered therapeutic efficacy.

summary: The serum concentration of Bisoprolol can be increased when it is combined with Morphine.
extended_description: The concomitant administration of two or more substrates of the p-gp transporter may result in the increased serum concentration of at least one or more of the substrates, due to competition among the substrates for the p-gp transporter. Depending on the p-gp substrate, this may result in drug toxicity and serious conditions such as excessive bleeding, cardiac conduction defects or organ toxicity, among many other effects.
management: Refer to individual product monographs for dosing adjustments and/or monitoring guidelines. Some combinations may be contraindicated .

Depending on the p-gp substrate, this may result in drug toxicity and serious conditions such as excessive bleeding, cardiac conduction defects or organ toxicity, among many other effects.
management: Refer to individual product monographs for dosing adjustments and/or monitoring guidelines. Some combinations may be contraindicated .

summary: The risk or severity of bleeding can be increased when Doxycycline is combined with Warfarin.
extended_description: There have been reports of prolonged prothrombin time in patients taking warfarin and doxycycline, increasing the risk of bleeding events. This is due to the fact that doxycycline has been shown to inhibit plasma prothrombin activity.
management: Patients who are on anticoagulant therapy may require a decrease in their anticoagulant dosage [F3085].

summary: Rabeprazole may increase the QTc-prolonging activities of Citalopram.
extended_description: Concomitant administration may decrease the metabolism of citalopram, which is metabolized by CYP2C19. This may increase the concentration of citalopram, leading to an increased risk of QT prolongation. The risk of this condition exists when citalopram is administered alone, and is further increased when CYP2C19 inhibitors are administered concomitantly.
management: 20 mg/day of citalopram is the maximum recommended dose for patients taking concomitant CYP2C19 inhibitors [F3301].

summary: The risk or severity of serotonin syndrome can be increased when Phenelzine is combined with Citalopram.
extended_description: During concomitant administration, there have been reports of serious and fatal reactions including hyperthermia, rigidity, myoclonus, autonomic instability with fluctuations of vital signs, and mental status changes progressing to delirium and coma. Some cases were noted to present features resembling serotonin syndrome [F3310]. Monoamine oxidase inhibitors inhibit the oxidation step of the citalopram metabolism pathway, increasing the serum concentration of citalopram, thereby increasing the risk of serotonin syndrome. Serotonin is a substrate of the monoamine oxidase inhibitors, therefore, due to decreased metabolism from inhibited monoamine oxidase enzymes, an increased level of serotonin is expected.
management: The FDA label for citalopram states that this combination is contraindicated. A 14-day washout period is suggested between the use of a monoamine oxidase inhibitor and citalopram.

summary: The serum concentration of Citalopram can be increased when it is combined with Cimetidine.
extended_description: In subjects who had received 21 days of 40 mg/day citalopram, combined administration of 400 mg/day cimetidine for 8 days resulted in an increase in citalopram AUC and Cmax of 43% and 39%, respectively [F3313]. Cimetidine inhibits the liver CYP450 enzyme metabolism of citalopram, increasing its exposure and potentiating the risk of QT prolongation among other toxicities.
management: Citalopram 20 mg/day is the maximum recommended dose for patients taking concomitant cimetidine due to the risk of QT prolongation [F3313].

summary: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Clarithromycin is combined with Amlodipine.
extended_description: Both drugs are substrates of CYP3A4 hepatic enzyme. Erythromycin or its similars may competitively inhibit this enzyme and lead to an increased serum concentration of amlodipine due to decreased metabolism, resulting in hypotension.
management: Avoid this combination when possible. Closely monitor for hypotension and bradycardia if this combination is administered.

summary: The metabolism of Amitriptyline can be decreased when combined with Cholecalciferol.
extended_description: Concomitant use of tricyclic antidepressants, such as amitriptyline, with drugs that can inhibit cytochrome P450 2D6, may require lower doses than normally prescribed for either the tricyclic antidepressant or the other drug. This is due to the fact that amitriptyline is metabolized by and inhibits CYP2D6, and other drugs that inhibit CYP2D6 may also be metabolized by this enzyme. This interaction can lead to an increase in drug exposure, toxicity, and adverse effects. Additionally, whenever one of these other drugs is withdrawn from concomitant therapy, an increased dose of amitriptyline may be required due to changes in metabolism.
management: The FDA label for amitriptyline recommends monitoring for amitriptyline plasma levels whenever it is coadministered with another drug known to be an inhibitor of P450 2D6 [FDA label]. Adjust dose as necessary, and ensure to monitor the plasma level of either drug after cessation of therapy of amitriptyline or the CYP2D6 inhibitors.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Lactulose can be decreased when used in combination with Calcium carbonate.
extended_description: Use of antacids may prevent the desired reduction in intestinal pH when using lactulose to treat or prevent hepatic encephalopathy, reducing the trapping of ammonia in the intestine. [F3622]
management: Avoid concomitant use of antacids with lactulose for the treatment or prevention of hepatic encephalopathy. If use cannot be avoided, monitor patient response to lactulose therapy.

summary: The therapeutic efficacy of Lactulose can be decreased when used in combination with Doxycycline.
extended_description: Orally administered anti-bacterial agents can kill intestinal bacteria thereby preventing the bacterial metabolism of lactulose and subsequent reduction in intestinal pH [F3622]. This can reduce the trapping of ammonia in the intestine.
management: Monitor the patient closely for therapeutic efficacy when using oral anti-bacterial agents and lactulose concomitantly. [F3622]
